parts.text: I looked at him, actually, for the first time. His hair was the color of the corn shocks and his eyes, very direct, were like the mountain sky when rain is pending-gray, with a shadowing of that miraculous blue.

questions.question: What color were the boy's eyes?
options: Gray with a shadowing of blue
questions.answer: Gray with a shadowing of blue
questions.explanation: The text compares his eyes to a mountain sky before rain: gray with miraculous blue.

parts.text: I gave him a quarter. 'You may come tomorrow afternoon,' I said, 'and thank you very much.' He looked at me, and at the coin, and seemed to want to speak, but could not, and turned away.

questions.question: The narrator paid the boy a ___ for his work.
questions.explanation: The text specifies that she gave him a quarter.

parts.text: At daylight I was half wakened by the sound of chopping. Again it was so even in texture that I went back to sleep. When I left my bed, the boy had come and gone, and a stack of kindling was neat against the cabin wall.

questions.question: Why did the narrator go back to sleep?
options: The chopping sound was even and steady
The boy told her to sleep
questions.answer: The chopping sound was even and steady
questions.explanation: The 'even texture' of the chopping sound was consistent enough that it didn't disturb her sleep.

parts.text: He came after school in the afternoon and worked until time to return to the orphanage. His name was Jerry.

questions.question: The boy, whose name was ___, worked after school.
questions.explanation: The narrator introduces the boy by name in this section.

parts.text: He had been at the orphanage since he was four. I could picture him at four, with the same grave gray-blue eyes and the same independence.

questions.question: How old was Jerry when he first came to the orphanage?
questions.explanation: The story mentions he had been there since he was four years old.

parts.text: No, the word that comes to me is 'integrity'. It is bedded on courage, but it is more than brave. It is honest, but it is more than honesty.
The ax handle broke one day. Jerry said the woodshop at the orphanage would repair it. I brought money to pay for the job and he refused it.

questions.question: What happened to the equipment Jerry was using?
questions.explanation: The text states 'The ax handle broke one day.'

parts.text: 'I'll pay for it,' he said. 'I broke it. I brought the ax down careless.' 'But no one hits accurately every time,' I told him. 'The fault was in the wood of the handle. I'll see the man from whom I bought it'

questions.question: Jerry insisted on paying because he claimed he was ___.
questions.explanation: Jerry takes responsibility by saying, 'I brought the ax down careless.'
